evidence: Correctness passed with zero error for all shapes.
Aggregate speedup 1.026x, but only d=3584 shows meaningful gain (1.107x); d=4096 and d=4608 are near baseline (0.997x, 0.982x).
Profile summary indicates bottleneck is rank-16 update or memory bandwidth.
Candidate uses shape dispatch: custom vectorized kernel for d<4096, ATen fallback for d>=4096. The fallback avoids regression but provides no speedup.
Custom kernel uses float4 vectorized access and grid-stride loop, which helps at d=3584 but is not applied to larger sizes.

recommended_actions: vary block size
try float4 stores when d is divisible by 4
fall back to ATen if numerical error appears
Investigate custom kernel performance at d=4096 and d=4608 to see if it can be tuned to beat baseline (e.g., adjust block size, shared memory usage for A/B).
Consider lowering the dispatch threshold if the custom kernel can be made faster for 4096.
Profile memory bandwidth utilization and occupancy for the custom kernel at larger sizes.
Ensure memory coalescing is maintained for all d values, especially when d is not a multiple of vector width.

candidate.name: fused_rank16_vectorized_grid_stride_dispatch
candidate.strategy: Fix grid-size overflow via grid-stride loop, use float4 vectorized access, and dispatch to ATen for d>=4096 to avoid regression; aggregate speedup may exceed 1.01x.

suggested_fixes: Add y = y.contiguous(); before launch_rank16_add(y, Ac, t, d);
warnings: The output tensor y from matmul is assumed contiguous; consider calling y = y.contiguous() before passing to the custom kernel to guarantee correct memory layout for vectorized access.

evidence: Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0) for all shapes.
Aggregate speedup is 1.01x, essentially identical to the PyTorch baseline.
Speedup per shape: 3584: 1.04x, 4096: 1.00x, 4608: 0.998x, indicating no meaningful improvement.
Profile summary identifies bottleneck as multiple ATen GEMM and add launches, which is exactly the baseline implementation.
The rank-16 update (A @ (B.T @ X)) involves two small GEMMs and an addition, causing launch overhead and memory bandwidth inefficiency.
The large W @ X GEMM is already handled by cuBLAS and is not the bottleneck.

recommended_actions: keep cuBLAS for W @ X
fuse rank-16 update into the output tensor
test scalar and vectorized rank-16 kernels
Fuse the rank-16 update into a single kernel: compute B.T @ X and then A @ result in one pass, writing directly to the output to avoid intermediate allocations and reduce kernel launches.
Keep cuBLAS for the large W @ X GEMM; do not attempt a custom full GEMM.
Consider vectorized memory accesses and unrolling for the fused rank-16 kernel to improve memory bandwidth utilization.

Profile to confirm that cuBLAS W @ X consumes >95% of time; if so, accept baseline as near-optimal.
If further optimization is required, explore overlapping the rank-16 update with the GEMM using a separate stream, but this adds complexity and may not help due to GPU saturation.

candidate.name: fused_rank16_kernel
candidate.strategy: Fuse A @ (B.T @ X) into a single kernel that writes directly to the output of W @ X, eliminating intermediate tensor allocations and reducing kernel launch overhead. Vectorized loads (float4) and shared memory tiling improve memory bandwidth utilization for the rank-16 update.

errors: Grid size exceeds maximum allowed (82944 > 65535) for 1D grid launch, causing potential kernel launch failure on GPUs with max grid size 65535.
suggested_fixes: Cap the number of blocks to 65535 and rely on the grid-stride loop to cover all elements, or use a 2D grid to distribute blocks.
warnings: Vectorized loads/stores assume 16-byte alignment without checking, which may lead to unaligned access and performance degradation or undefined behavior on some architectures.
